translations.source: No country remains untouched from this issue, and WHO has received reports of substandard or falsified medical medicines, vaccines and in vitro diagnostics from all regions of the world. Both generic and innovator medicines can be falsified, ranging from very expensive products for cancer to very inexpensive products for treatment of pain. WHO Member States have agreed on a comprehensive global strategy focused on prevention, detection and response to move towards achieving increased access to quality, safe, effective and quality medical products. Impact: Substandard and falsified medical products create many problems for both the individual taking the medicine and for families, the larger society, health care system and supply chain. When medicines do not work the way they should, they can fail to treat or prevent disease, contribute to antimicrobial resistance, waste precious resources and lead to a loss of confidence in medicines and distrust of healthcare providers.
translations.reference: Kò sí orílẹ̀ èdè tí kò tíì faragbá nínú ọ̀ràn yìí, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni WHO gba àwọn ìròyìn òògùn, abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti irinṣẹ́ àyẹ̀wò àìsàn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ ní gbogbo àwọn ẹkùn àgbáyé. Àti àwọn òògùn abéròjàjórúkọ àti àwọn òògùn àtọọ́dá ni wọ́n ṣe é gbélẹ̀rọ, láti orí orí àwọn ohun èlò olówó gọbọi fún jẹjẹrẹ dórí àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n kò wọ́n rárá fún ìtọ́jú ìrora. Àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé fẹnukò lórí ọgbọ́n gbòòrò káríayé tí ó fojúsun ìdènà, àyẹ̀wò àti ìfèsì láti tẹ̀síwájú lórí àṣeyọrí níní àǹfààní púpọ̀ sí ohùn èlò ìṣègùn dídára, aláàbò, tí ó múnádóko tí ó sì dára. Ipa: Àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ ṣẹ̀dá ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìṣòro fún ati awon ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń lo àwọn òògùn náà àti fún àwọn ẹbí, àwùjọ gbòòrò, ètò ìtọ́jú ilera àti ẹ̀wọ́n ìpèsè. Nígbà tí àwọn òògùn kò bá ṣiṣẹ́ bí ó ṣe yẹ kí wọ́n ṣiṣẹ́, wọ́n lè kùnà láti ṣèwòsàn tàbí dènà àìsàn, ń dá kún ìlòdìsí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò, ìfiṣòfò àwọn àlùmọ́ọ́nì iyebíye tí yóò sì yọrí sí àdánù ìgboyà nínú lílo àwọn òògùn àti àìfọkàntán àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera.

Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ó jẹ́ àwọn ọmọbìnú WHO ti fẹ́ràn sí ìlànà àgbègbè tó pọ̀ ní ìtóju, ìfamọ́, àti itumọ̀ fún ìgbésí ayé tí ó ní ìpọ̀lù sí ìdahun àti àṣàyìnú àwọn ọjà ìwọ̀ tí ó ní ìṣọ̀rọ̀, ńlá, àfiún àti àṣàyìnú. lóòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòòò translations.source: This can have a negative socioeconomic impact, contributing to related loss in productivity and adding expenses to the individual and national health system. They are most likely to reach patients in situations where there is constrained access to quality and safe medical products, poor governance and weak technical capacity. Although this issue impacts all countries, countries where there are weak or non-existent regulatory systems bear the greatest burden of substandard and falsified medical products. In an increasingly globalized world, no one country has sufficient resources and capacity to deal with this issue in isolation. Through global systems and processes such as the WHO Global Surveillance and Monitoring System and WHO Member State Mechanism,
translations.reference: Èyí lè ní ipa ajẹmọ́ àwùjọ òun ọrọ̀ ajé òdì, níní ipa lórí àdánù tí ó ní í ṣe nínú ìpèsè àti ṣíṣe àfikún àwọn owónàá fún ènìyàn náà àti ètò ìlera orílẹ̀ èdè. Ó ṣe é ṣe kí wọ́n kàn sí àwọn aláìsàn ní àwọn ipò níbi tí ìdènà àǹfààní ti wà sí àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn dídára àti aláàbò, ìdarí tí kò dára àti ipa ìmọ̀ tí kò lágbára. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ọ̀ràn yìí ń nípa lórí gbogbo orílẹ̀ èdè, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè níbi tí wọ́n kò ti ètò ìdarí aláìlágbára tàbí tí kò sí ti ń rú àjàgà púpọ̀ àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Ní ayé tí ó ti lajú sí i, kò sí orílẹ̀ èdè tí ó ní àwọn èròjà tí ó tó àti agbára láti gbógun ti ọ̀ràn náà ní àdáwà. Nípasẹ̀ àwọn ètò àti ìgbésẹ̀ àgbáyé bí Ètò Àkóso àti Àmójútó Káríayé ẹgbẹ́ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé pẹ̀lú ọ̀ràn yìí ní àdáwà.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's a translation of the provided English text into Yoruba, aiming for accuracy and natural flow. I've included explanations after the translation to clarify some choices and potential nuances.

**Yoruba Translation:**

"Eyí le pọ̀ sí ipò́ èkó-ọ̀gójì tí ó ní àfiífẹ̀ẹ́, tó ṣe àfihànnù dé ìdàgbádò tó ní àfiífẹ̀ẹ́ àti fifun ìmọ̀ àfiífẹ̀ẹ́ fún àwọn ènìyàn àti ilẹ̀ gbogbo. Wọ́n yà lẹ́nu láti de ọ̀jọ̀gbọn ní àwọn àwọn ìṣurobìn tó ní àfiífẹ̀ẹ́ láti gbé àwọn èwé ìwò tó ní ìṣọ̀rọ̀ àti àáyà, ìdarí tó ní ìbàjẹ́, àti àgbọ́yè tèkníkì tó ní ìbàjẹ́. Bóòlẹ̀ẹ́, àfiífẹ̀ẹ́ yìí ń tàn ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè gbogbo, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tó ní àwọn ọ̀gáwé ètò tẹriba tí ó ní ìbàjẹ́ jùbọ́ jẹ́ óòò ní ìbàjẹ́ tí ó pọ̀ ní ìwàdìya àti àwọn èwé ìwò tí ó ń bẹ́rẹ́. Ní àgbègbè àgbágbé tó ní ìlú ilẹ̀, orílẹ̀-èdè kan kò ní láaye láti fi ìrànlọ́wọ́ àti àgbọ́yè láti fi dojú ìwàdìya yìí ní àfiífẹ̀ẹ́. Láàárín àwọn ọ̀gáwé ọ̀túnà àgbágbé àti ìwè ìwájú bí WHO Global Surveillance and Monitoring System àti WHO Member State Mechanism,"

**Explanation of Choices and Nuances:**

*   **"Eyí le pọ̀ sí..."** - "This can lead to..."  This is a common way to express possibility and consequence in Yoruba.
*   **"ìpò́ èkó-ọ̀gójì tí ó ní àfiífẹ̀ẹ́"** - "negative socioeconomic impact."  I've used "ìpò́ èkó-ọ̀gójì" for socioeconomic impact. "Tí ó ní àfiífẹ̀ẹ́" means "that has a negative effect/impact."
*   **"tó ṣe àfihànnù dé..."** - "contributing to related loss..."  "Àfihànnù" means contributing or leading to.
*   **"ìdàgbádò tó ní àfiífẹ̀ẹ́"** - "loss in productivity."
*   **"fifun ìmọ̀ àfiífẹ̀ẹ́ fún àwọn ènìyàn àti ilẹ̀ gbogbo"** - "adding expenses to the individual and national health system."  This is a more literal translation, ensuring clarity.
*   **"Wọ́n yà lẹ́nu láti de ọ̀jọ̀gbọn..."** - "They are most likely to reach patients..." "Yà lẹ́nu" means "are likely" or "are most likely." "Ọ̀jọ̀gbọn" means patients.
*   **"àwọn ìṣurobìn tó ní àfiífẹ̀ẹ́ láti gbé àwọn èwé ìwò tó ní ìṣọ̀rọ̀ àti àáyà"** - "situations where there is constrained access to quality and safe medical products." This is a complex phrase, and I've tried to capture the meaning accurately. "Ìṣurobìn" means situation. "Gbé" means to have or possess. "Èwé ìwò" means medical products. "Ìṣọ̀rọ̀" means quality and "àáyà" means safety.
*   **"ìdarí tó ní ìbàjẹ́"** - "poor governance." "Ìdarí" means governance and "ní ìbàjẹ́" means with problems/weak.
*   **"àgbọ́yè tèkníkì tó ní ìbàjẹ́"** - "weak technical capacity."
*   **"Bóòlẹ̀ẹ́"** - "Although"
*   **"àwọn ọ̀gáwé ètò tẹriba tí ó ní ìbàjẹ́ jùbọ́"** - "countries where there are weak or non-existent regulatory systems." "Ètò tẹriba" means regulatory system.
*   **"jẹ́ óòò ní ìbàjẹ́ tí ó pọ̀ ní ìwàdìya àti àwọn èwé ìwò tí ó ń bẹ́rẹ́"** - "bear the greatest burden of substandard and falsified medical products."
*   **"Ní àgbègbè àgbágbé tó ní ìlú ilẹ̀"** - "In an increasingly globalized world."
*   **"orílẹ̀-èdè kan kò ní láaye láti fi ìrànlọ́wọ́ àti àgbọ́yè láti fi dojú ìwàdìya yìí ní àfiífẹ̀ẹ́"** - "no one country has sufficient resources and capacity to deal with this issue in isolation."
*   **"Láàárín àwọn ọ̀gáwé ọ̀túnà àgbágbé àti ìwè ìwájú"** - "Through global systems and processes."

**Important Considerations:**

*   **Context is Key:**  The best translation always depends on the specific context. If you can provide more information about where this text will be used, I can refine the translation further.
*   **Yoruba Dialects:** Yoruba has several dialects. This translation aims for a generally understandable form.
*   **Technical Terminology:**  Medical and socioeconomic terminology can be challenging. I've used the most appropriate terms I know, but there might be alternative phrasing depending on the target audience.

I hope this translation is helpful! Please let me know if you have any questions or would like me to make any adjustments.

translations.source: Member States are working together to prevent, detect and respond to substandard and falsified medical products.
translations.reference: Àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti dènà, ṣàyẹ̀wò àti fèsì sí àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's the translation of "Member States are working together to prevent, detect and respond to substandard and falsified medical products" into Yoruba, along with a few options to consider depending on the desired level of formality and nuance.  I'll provide explanations after each option.

**Option 1 (More Formal & Precise - Recommended):**

"Awọn orilẹ-ede oniṣandiwáa ń ṣiṣẹ́ nìgbẹ̀gbe láti ṣe àfiẹ́kàn, wáà mú àti ṣe aṣáyìn sí iṣẹ̀dá áti áwọn ọ́gbọ̀n iṣòrò ti òrúré."

*   **Awọn orilẹ-ede oniṣandiwáa:** Member States (literally "the countries of the member states") - This is a standard and accurate way to refer to member states in a formal context.
*   **ń ṣiṣẹ́ nìgbẹ̀gbe:** are working together (literally "are working together")
*   **láti ṣe àfiẹ́kàn:** to prevent (literally "to do prevention")
*   **wáà mú:** to detect (literally "to find/discover")
*   **àti ṣe aṣáyìn sí:** and respond to (literally "and do a response to")
*   **iṣẹ̀dá áti áwọn ọ́gbọ̀n iṣòrò ti òrúré:** substandard and falsified medical products.  (This is a more detailed and accurate rendering.  *Iṣẹ̀dá* means substandard, *áwọn ọ́gbọ̀n iṣòrò* means falsified products, and *ti òrúré* means medical/health-related).

**Option 2 (Slightly Less Formal, Still Accurate):**

"Awọn orilẹ-ede ń ṣiṣẹ́ nìgbẹ̀gbe láti dàákàn, wáà mú àti ṣe aṣáyìn sí iṣẹ̀dá áti áwọn ọ́gbọ̀n iṣòrò iṣòrò ti òrúré."

*   This is very similar to Option 1, but uses "dàákàn" instead of "sè àfiẹ́kàn" for "prevent."  Both are acceptable, but "dàákàn" is slightly more common in everyday speech.

**Option 3 (More Simplified - Use with Caution):**

"Awọn orilẹ-ede ń ṣiṣẹ́ nìgbẹ̀gbe láti yàto, wáà mú àti ṣe aṣáyìn sí áwọn ọ́gbọ̀n iṣòrò ti òrúré ti kò sé dàára."

*   **yàto:** to prevent (a more general term, less precise than "dàákàn" or "sè àfiẹ́kàn")
*   **ti kò sé dàára:** which are not good/of poor quality (This replaces the more specific "substandard" with a more general description.  While understandable, it loses some of the original meaning's precision.)

**Important Considerations for Low-Resource Languages:**

*   **Terminology:**  Medical terminology can be tricky. I've tried to use the most accurate and commonly understood terms.  If this translation is for a specific audience (e.g., healthcare professionals), it might be necessary to consult with a Yoruba-speaking medical expert to ensure the terms are appropriate.
*   **Context:** The best translation always depends on the context.  Is this for a formal report, a public health announcement, or something else?
*   **Dialect:** Yoruba has different dialects. The above translations use a fairly standard form, but there might be slight variations depending on the specific region.



**Therefore, I recommend Option 1 as the most accurate and appropriate translation for most situations.**  Please let me know if you have any other questions or need further refinement!

translations.source: Suicide prevention Overview: More than 700 000 people die by suicide every year. For each suicide, there are an estimated more than 20 suicide attempts. Suicide can occur at any stage of life and in all regions of the world. In 2019, suicide was the fourth leading cause of death among 15–29-year-olds globally, and 77% of all suicides happened in low- and middle-income countries.
translations.reference: Ìdènà ìgbẹ́míaraẹni Ìsọnísókí: Ó lé ní àwọn ènìyàn 700 000 ni wọ́n kú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lọ́dọọdún. Fún ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan, ìṣirò àwọn ìgbìyànjú láti gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni tí ó ju 20 ló wà. Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lè wáyé ní èyíkéyìí ìpele ìgbésí ayé àti ní gbogbo ẹkùn àgbáyé. Ní 2019, ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni asíwájú okùnfà ikú kẹrin láàrin àwọn ọmọ ọdún 15-29 káàkiri àgbáyé, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni 77% gbogbo àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti àti elérè gọbọi.

**Yoruba Translation:**

Imọran Ìfẹ́ (Suicide Prevention) ni: Diẹ sii ju 700,000 eniyan lo o ku nitori ìmọran ífẹ́ papọ̀ ní gbogbo ọdún. Fun gbogbo ìmọran ífẹ́ kan tí o ku, a ní ìborí pé o jẹ́ pípẹ̀ẹ̀ jùlọ̀ 20 àwọn ìnkọ̀kò ìmọran ífẹ́. Ìmọran ífẹ́ le ṣiṣẹ́ ní ìgbà eyígbà ti ayé àti ní gbogbo awọn agbo ti àgbègbè ní ayé. Ní ọdún 2019, ìmọran ífẹ́ jẹ́ ohun akọkọ́ tí o ku di mẹrin lára àwọn tó ní 15–29 ọdún àgbọ̀yọ̀gbọ̀n ní ayé gbogbo, àti pé 77% ti àwọn ìmọran ífẹ́ gùn-gùn ti o ṣẹlẹ nínú àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ó ní ogo kekere àti ogo mọ́ọ́dá.

translations.source: Almost 20% of global suicides were due to pesticide self-poisoning. While the link between suicide and mental health conditions (in particular, depression and alcohol use disorders) and a previous suicide attempt is well established in high-income countries, many suicides happen impulsively in moments of crisis. Further risk factors for suicide include experience of loss, loneliness, discrimination, a relationship break-up, financial problems, chronic pain and illness, violence, abuse, and conflict or other humanitarian emergencies. Stigma around mental health conditions and suicide means many people thinking about suicide do not seek help. Suicides and suicide attempts have a ripple effect that impacts on families, friends, colleagues, communities and societies.
translations.reference: Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ẹ́ tó 20% àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lágbàáyé ṣẹlẹ̀ láti ara mímu májèlé òògùn apako. Nígbà tí àjọṣepọ̀ láàrin ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìṣòro ìlera orí (pàápàá jùlọ, ìbànújẹ́ ọkàn àti ìṣòro ọtí mímu) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni tẹ́lẹ̀ fẹsẹ̀ múlẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lásìkò ìṣòro. Àwọn okùnfà ewu fún ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni ìrírí àdánù, ìdáwà, ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà, ìyapa ibáraẹniṣepọ̀, ìṣòro owó, ìrora ńlá àti àìsàn, ìwà ipá, àti ìjà tàbí àwọn pàjáwìrì ajẹmọ́ ọmọnìyàn mìíràn. Ìdẹ́yẹsí tí ó yí ìṣòro ilerat orí ká àti ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni túmọ̀ sí pé ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń ronú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kò wá ìrànlọ́wọ́. Àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìgbìyànjú láti gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni ní agbára tí ó ń nípa lórí ẹbí, ọ̀rẹ́, alábàáṣiṣẹ́pọ̀, àwùjọ àti ìlú.

translations.source: Suicide: facts and figures globally Suicide worldwide in 2019 report LIVE LIFE: an implementation guide for suicide prevention in countries Prevention: Much can be done at individual, community and national levels to prevent suicide and suicide attempts. WHO’s LIVE LIFE approach recommends four effective, evidence-based key interventions which should be included in every national response to suicide:
translations.reference: Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni: àwọn òtítọ́ àti iye káàkiri àgbáyé Ìròyìn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni káàkiri àgbáyé fún 2019 GBÉ AYÉ: ṣíṣe àmúlò ìtọ́nisọ́nà funt ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè Ìdènà: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ nǹkan lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe ní àwọn ìpele ẹ̀dá kọ̀ọ̀kan, àwùjọ àti orílẹ̀ èdè láti dènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni. Ìlànà GBÉ AYÉ àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé dábàá àwọn ìpèsè mẹ́rin pàtàkì tí wọ́n múnádóko, adálórí-ẹ̀rí èyí tí ó gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ fífi sí ara ìtọ́jú orílẹ̀ èdè kọ̀ọ̀kan dórí ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni:

translations.source: Suicide prevention efforts require coordination and collaboration among multiple sectors, including health, education, labour, agriculture, business, justice, law, defence, politics and the media. Suicide prevention is often not adequately addressed because of stigma and lack of awareness that suicide is a major public health problem. Communities also play a crucial role in preventing suicide. The WHO community engagement toolkit guides communities in identifying priorities and implementing activities according to local contexts. WHO response: The urgency to act to prevent suicides has been recognized and prioritized at the highest levels.
translations.reference: Àwọn akitiyan ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni pè fún àkóso àti àjọṣepọ̀ láàrin àwọn ọ̀pọ̀ ẹ̀ka, tí ó fi mọ́ ìlera, ẹ̀kọ́, iṣẹ́, iṣẹ́ àgbẹ̀, okòwò, ìdájọ́, òfin, òṣèlú àti iṣẹ́ ìròyìn. Ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kì í sáábà jẹ́ wíwá ojútùú sí nítorí ìdẹ́yẹsí àti àìsí ìpolongo pé ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ ńlá. Àwọn àwùjọ náà ń kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni. Irinṣẹ́ àmúlò ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àwùjọ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ń ṣe ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwọn àwùjọ láti ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn nǹkan pàtàkì àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn iṣẹ́ gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn ọ̀gangan ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ abẹ́lé. Èsì WHO: Ìpòǹgbẹ láti ṣiṣẹ́ dènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni wọ́n ti ṣe ìdámọ̀ tí wọ́n sì ti sọ di pàtàkì ní àwọn ìpele gíga jùlọ.

translations.source: The reduction of suicide rates in countries is an indicator in the UN Sustainable Development Goals (the only indicator for mental health), WHO’s General Programme of Work and WHO’s Comprehensive Mental Health Action Plan 2013–2030. The first WHO world suicide report, Preventing suicide: a global imperative, published in 2014, aimed to increase awareness of the public health significance of suicide and suicide attempts and make suicide prevention a public health priority. Governments are encouraged to implement WHO’s LIVE LIFE package of effective and evidence-based key interventions for suicide prevention, and to develop or strengthen comprehensive national suicide prevention strategies in a multisectoral public health approach. National leadership and coordination on suicide prevention are further supported by the publication of National suicide prevention strategies: progress, examples and indicators. Recognizing that data and statistics are necessary for advocacy and decision-making, WHO published Suicide worldwide in 2019: Global Health Estimates.
translations.reference: Àdínkù ìwọ̀n ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè jẹ́ ìtọ́ka nínú àwọn Àfojúsùn ti UN (ìtọ́ka fún ìlera orí kan ṣoṣo), Ètò Gbogbogbò Iṣẹ́ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé àti Ètò Iṣẹ́ Ìlera orí Gbòòrò 2013-2030. Ìròyìn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àgbáyé kìíní ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé, Ìdènà Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni: ìmọ̀ abágbàmu káríayé, tí wọ́n tẹ̀ jáde ní 2014, tí ó ní àfojúsùn láti mú ìgbéga bá ìpolongo pàtàkì ìlera àwùjọ ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti àwọn ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni àti sísọ ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni di pàtàkì ìlera àwùjọ. Wọ́n gba àwọn ìjọba ní ìyànjú láti ṣàmúlò àgbékalẹ̀ GBÉ AYÉ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ti àwọn ìdásí pàtàkì pípé àti adálórí-ẹ̀rí fún ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti láti mú ìdàgbàsókè tàbí ìrólágbára bá àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àgbáyé gbígbòòrò nínú ìlànà ìlera àwùjọ ọlọ́pọ̀ ẹ̀ka. Ìdàrí àti àkóso àgbáyé lórí ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni wọ́n túbọ̀ ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún nípa àtẹ̀jáde àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni orílẹ̀ èdè: ìlọsíwájú, àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti ìtọ́ka. Ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ ìwífún àlàyé àti ìṣirò hebt fún ìkéde àti ṣíṣe ìpinnu, àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé tẹ Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni jákèjádò àgbáyé ní 2019: Àwọn Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the Yoruba translation of the provided document, aiming for accuracy and natural flow. I've included explanations of some choices afterward to clarify the translation process.

**Ìtọsi ìdàkùkù àgbàlagbà tí ó ní ìlú-ilú jẹ́ àfihan nínú Apẹrẹ Àṣàwọ̀ Àgbègbè ti Apapo Orílẹ̀-èdè (òun nìkan tó ní àfihan fún ilera ìgbàgbé), Iṣẹ́ Ìlànà Ìlú WHO àti Èsìn Ilera Ìgbàgbé WHO 2013–2030. Àkọ́ṣẹ́ WHO lára àgbáyé fún ìdàkùkù, Ìfọwọ́ṣe ìdàkùkù: Àṣàwọ̀ gbogbo, tó kàn ni 2014, fúnrànán láti pọ̀ níbẹ̀yìn sí àwọn èmí ilera àgbàlagbà ti ìdàkùkù àti àwọn ìgbala ìdàkùkù, àti láti ṣe ìdàkùkù níbi ilera àgbàlagbà. A máa nífẹ̀ẹ́ láti fi WHO’s LIVE LIFE package ti àwọn ìforukọ́ṣe àti àwọn ìṣàdúróòyìn tí ó ní àmúù àfiwégbá fún ìdàkùkù, àti láti ṣíṣẹ́ tàbí láti mú ìlànà ìdàkùkù àgbègbè tó pọ̀pù ní ìtóju ilera àgbàlagbà. A fi ìtọsi àti àṣàwọ̀ nípa ìdàkùkù mọ́ àkọ́kòó nígbà tí a kàn National suicide prevention strategies: progress, examples and indicators. Nígbà tí a mọ̀ pé àwọn ẹ̀̀sìn àti àwọn àkọ́ṣe jẹ́ àwọn ohun tí ó ní láaye láti ṣe ìtan fún àwọn ẹ̀̀sìn àti ìyẹ́wú, WHO kàn Suicide worldwide in 2019: Global Health Estimates.**

**Explanation of Choices and Considerations:**

*   **Terminology:** I've tried to use established Yoruba terms where possible. For example, "suicide" is translated as "ìdàkùkù." "Mental health" is "ilera ìgbàgbé."
*   **Formal Language:** The original document is formal, so the translation maintains a formal tone.
*   **Complex Sentences:** The English text contains long, complex sentences. I've broken some of these down into shorter, more manageable sentences in Yoruba to improve readability.
*   **"WHO's LIVE LIFE package":**  I've kept "WHO's LIVE LIFE package" as is, as it's a specific program name.  It's common practice to retain proper nouns and program names in their original form.
*   **Multisectoral:** Translated as "tó pọ̀pù ní ìtóju ilera àgbàlagbà" (comprehensive in public health approach).
*   **"Advocacy and decision-making":** Translated as "ìtan fún àwọn ẹ̀̀sìn àti ìyẹ́wú" (to advocate for actions and decisions).
*   **Accuracy vs. Flow:** I've prioritized accuracy while also trying to ensure the translation reads naturally in Yoruba.  There's always a balance to be struck.

**Important Notes for Low-Resource Languages:**

*   **Context is Key:**  Translation for low-resource languages often requires a deep understanding of the cultural context.
*   **Collaboration:** Ideally, a translation like this would be reviewed by a native Yoruba speaker with expertise in public health terminology.
*   **Glossary Development:** For ongoing translation projects, creating a glossary of key terms is essential to ensure consistency.
*   **Iterative Process:** Translation is rarely perfect on the first try. It's an iterative process of review and refinement.



I hope this translation is helpful! Let me know if you have any other documents you'd like me to translate.

translations.source: WHO’s vision is a world where death by suicide is no longer a leading cause of death and where people who are attempting or thinking about suicide feel comfortable seeking help which is provided capably and respectfully to all.
translations.reference: Àfojúsùn àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ni ayé tí ikú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kò ti síwájú okùnfà ikú àti níbi tí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbìyànjú tàbí ronú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ti lè rí ìrọ̀rùn láti béèrè fún ìrànlọ́wọ́ èyí tí wọ́n ń pèsè nínú ipá àti ọ̀wọ̀ fún gbogbo ènìyàn.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the translation of the provided English text into Yoruba, aiming for accuracy and natural flow:

**Yoruba Translation:**

Aseje vision ti WHO ni aye ti o gbagbo wipe ileku ti o yọ apata ni ko maa n lo bi owo die die ti ileku, ati pe awon eniyan ti n gbiyanju tabi o mo oore ti o yọ apata yio ma ronu pe o le ra gbe jade ati pe iranlọwọ yio ma pese fun gbogbo eniyan ni ipo ti o tọ ati ti a nifẹẹẹ.

**Explanation of Choices & Nuances:**

*   **"WHO’s vision"**: "Aseje vision ti WHO" - "Aseje" is used to indicate "vision" or "goal" in this context. "Ti" means "of".
*   **"is a world where..."**: "ni aye ti o gbagbo wipe..." -  "ni aye" means "in a world". "gbagbo wipe" means "where it is known/understood that".
*   **"death by suicide is no longer a leading cause of death"**: "ileku ti o yọ apata ni ko maa n lo bi owo die die ti ileku" - This is a more literal translation, ensuring clarity. "ileku" means "death". "ti o yọ apata" means "by suicide". "ko maa n lo" means "will no longer be". "owo die die" means "leading/major".
*   **"people who are attempting or thinking about suicide"**: "awon eniyan ti n gbiyanju tabi o mo oore ti o yọ apata yio ma ronu" - "awon eniyan" means "people". "ti n gbiyanju" means "who are attempting". "tabi" means "or". "o mo oore" means "they know/feel the urge".
*   **"feel comfortable seeking help"**: "yio ma ronu pe o le ra gbe jade" - "yio ma ronu" means "will think/feel". "o le ra gbe jade" means "they can come out/seek help".
*   **"which is provided capably and respectfully to all"**: "ati pe iranlọwọ yio ma pese fun gbogbo eniyan ni ipo ti o tọ ati ti a nifẹẹẹ." - "iranlọwọ" means "help". "yio ma pese" means "will be provided". "fun gbogbo eniyan" means "to all people". "ni ipo ti o tọ ati ti a nifẹẹẹ" means "in a proper and respectful manner".



I hope this translation is helpful! Let me know if you have any other documents you'd like me to translate.

translations.source: Sustainable development Overview: Sustainable development is a broad term to describe policies, projects and investments that provide benefits today without sacrificing environmental, social and personal health in the future. These policies are often described as green because they focus on limiting the impact of development on the environment. However, the benefits of sustainable development are also felt across a wide cross section of human health and well-being, including reductions in pollution- and environment-related disease, improved health outcomes and decreased stress. As the threats of climate change become increasingly important, win-win strategies for mitigation, health improvement and cost savings offer a range of advantages for various stakeholders.
translations.reference: Ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése Ìsọnísókí: Ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése ni ọ̀rọ̀ ìperí gbòòrò láti ṣàpèjúwe àwọn ìlànà, iṣẹ́ àkànṣe àti ìdókòwò tí ó ń pèsè àwọn àǹfààní lónìí láì kọlu ìlera àyíká, àwùjọ àti ti ara ẹni lọ́jọ́ iwájú. Àwọn ìlànà wọ̀nyí ni wọ́n sáábà máa ń ṣàpèjúwe gẹ́gẹ́ bí àìléwu nítorí pé wọ́n fojúsùn mímú àdínkù bá ipa ìdàgbàsókè lórí àyíká. Àmọ́ sá, àwọn àǹfààní ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése ń jẹ́ rírí káàkiri àwọn onírúurú ẹ̀ka ìlera àti àlàáfíà ọmọnìyàn, tí ó fi mọ́ àdínkù abayíkájẹ́- àti àìsàn adálórí àyíká, àwọn àbájáde ìlera tí ó lọ síwájú àti àdínkù ìṣòro. Bí ìṣòro àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ ṣe ń ṣe pàtàkì sí i, àwọn ọgbọ́n àmúṣiṣẹ́ fún àdínkù ìbàjẹ́, ìtẹ̀síwájú ìlera àti díndín iyeó kù ń pèsè oríṣiríṣi àǹfààní fún àwọn onírúurú àwọn àjọ.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's a translation of the provided English text into Yoruba, aiming for clarity and accuracy while considering the nuances of the language. I'll also include some notes afterward explaining some of the choices made.

**Yoruba Translation:**

Imudojukojo Imudara: Imudojukojo imudara jẹ adari ti o tobi ti o ṣe apejọ awọn iṣuro, awọn idanirele, ati awọn idoko-owo ti o nifẹ awọn anfani lọwọ loni, paapaa laisi ṣiṣe awọn ire kọja ti ayin-yin, iyara, ati ilera ti ara ni ọjọ iwaju.  Awọn iṣuro wọ̀nyí maṣę lẹnu pe “lẹ́ẹ̀rin” nitori wọn se daradara ni iṣọọgún iṣọ̀ro iyíàrúnṣọ̀pọ̀ ti idagba lori ayin-yin. Sibẹ́sibí, awọn anfani ti imudojukojo imudara tun ni ipa lori pupọ̀ ni ilera ara ati alafia, pẹlú ṣiṣiṣe awọn ilara ti o ni ibatan si ilẹ̀dàpọ̀ ati ayin-yin, ilera ti o ṣókàn, ati ṣiṣiṣe àṣàdìya. Bí ó ti ṣe ilé ayé ti yàká ní ìlànà àṣà, àwọn ilana ti o mú ìrànlọ́wọ̀, ilera, àti àwọn ìdàgbádògbé níwájú náà nfunni inú àwọn ìgbádúrà tó ọpẹ́ lọ́wọ́ àwọn olùkọ̀ọ̀gẹ́édé.

translations.source: For example, greener health care operations can generate patient and worker health benefits while also saving energy, mitigating climate risks and creating long-term cost savings. The World Health Organization is committed to pursuing sustainable development in all its work to help protect the people of tomorrow from the health growing health risks of today. Impact: Many sustainable development strategies can offer significant wins for health, climate and the environment, and the benefits can be seen almost immediately. For example, many health and development goals can be achieved simultaneously by tackling air pollution, which is responsible for an estimated 7 million premature deaths annually. Polluting emissions include powerful short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)—particularly black carbon, ozone and methane—which are very harmful to health but have only brief atmospheric lifetimes.
translations.reference: Fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera kánrinkése lè ṣẹ̀dá àwọn àǹfààní ìlera aláìsàn àti òṣìṣẹ́ nígbà tí wọ́n tún ń pa agbára mọ́, ṣe àdínkù ewu ojú ọjọ́ àti ìṣẹ̀dá àdínkù iyeó ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera àgbáyé ti pinnu láti lépa ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése nínú gbogbo iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ dènà àwọn ènìyàn ọjọ́ iwájú lọ́wọ́ àwọn ewu ìlera ọjọ́ òní tí wọ́n ń dàgbà. Ipa: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése lè pèsè àwọn àṣeyọrí pàtàkì fún ìlera, ojú ọjọ́ àti àyíká, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àǹfààní rẹ̀ lè jẹ́ rírí lẹ́sẹ̀kẹsẹ̀. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àfojúsùn ìlera àti ìdàgbàsókè lè jẹ́ àṣeyọrí papọ̀ nípa gbígbógun ti ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́, èyí tí ó ń ṣokùnfà ìṣirò mílíọ̀nù 7 ikú àìtọ́jọ́ lọ́dọọdún. Lára àwọn ohun abàyíkájẹ́ ni àwọn abojú-ọjọ́-jẹ́ alágbára ọlọ́jọ́ díẹ̀ (SLCPs) – pàápàá jùlọ èédú dúdú, òyì àrá àti gáàsì mẹtíìnì– tí wọ́n léwu púpọ̀ fún ìlera ṣùgbọ́n àsìkò ìsẹ̀mí wọn kì í pẹ́.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's a translation of the provided English text into Yoruba, aiming for clarity and natural flow. I've included some notes afterward explaining some of the choices made.

**Yoruba Translation:**

Fun oriire, awọn iṣẹ́ ilera ti o lagbara ni idagba le mu awọn anfani ilera ti awọn alákùmbá àti àwọn olùjọba diẹ sii, nígbà tí ó máa ń ṣàfí ẹ̀rọ, ṣí iṣàgbògbọ́n ayíjó àti ṣí iṣàwóchèkò àwọn ohun ìrànlọ́wọ́ lọ́wọ́. Àjọ Àgbàgbẹ́ Ilera Agbẹẹ́ (World Health Organization) ni ìtọ́ju fún ìdàgbádò ọ̀túnà lọ́wọ́ ní gbogbo àwọn ìforúkan rẹ̀ láti ràn án lọ́wọ́ láti ṣí ìwà àti àṣàyìń òwúrọ̀ àgbẹ̀wọ̀n ilera tí ó ń tàn yà lónìí. Àpọ̀p̣ọ̀: Àwọn ilana ìdàgbádò ọ̀túnà púpọ̀ le mu àwọn ìgbádákùnrin tó ṣe pataki fún ilera, ayíjó àti àgbẹ̀wọ̀n, àti àwọn anfani le rí nìkan láìsán ìrànlọ́wọ́. Fun àlàálẹ́, àwọn ìtemíìlé ilera àti ìdàgbádò púpọ̀ le ṣiṣẹp̣ẹ́ nígbà yìí nígbà tí a ba ṣàfí àṣàn ìgbàgbé, eyí tí ó ní ìdáná ti 7 milionu àwọn ìgbà ìdùn tó kúnrẹ́rẹ́ nígbà ọdún kan. Àwọn ìlànà àṣàn pọ̀ ní àwọn àgbọ́ṣe àṣàn tí ó lagbara àti àìtúnà ayíjó (SLCPs)—pàtịpà àwọn ẹ̀̀ka àwọ̀ didun, ózóòń àti mítánì—wọn jẹ́ àìṣe láti ilera ṣugbọn ní àwọn ìgbà tí ó kúnrẹ́rẹ́ ní ayíjó.

**Notes on Translation Choices:**

*   **"Greener health care operations"**: Translated as "awọn iṣẹ́ ilera ti o lagbara ni idagba" (health care operations that are strong in growth/development).  This captures the idea of environmentally friendly and improving operations.
*   **"World Health Organization"**:  I used the full name "Àjọ Àgbàgbẹ́ Ilera Agbẹẹ́" for clarity, as it's the standard Yoruba translation.
*   **"Sustainable development"**: Translated as "ìdàgbádò ọ̀túnà" (sustainable development).
*   **"Climate risks"**: Translated as "ṣí iṣàgbògbọ́n ayíjó" (climate risks).
*   **"Short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)"**: I kept the acronym SLCPs and provided a descriptive translation in parentheses: "àwọn àgbọ́ṣe àṣàn tí ó lagbara àti àìtúnà ayíjó."
*   **"Premature deaths"**: Translated as "àwọn ìgbà ìdùn tó kúnrẹ́rẹ́" (shortened lives/premature deaths).
*   **Flow and Naturalness:** I've tried to structure the sentences in a way that sounds natural in Yoruba, rather than a direct word-for-word translation.  This sometimes involves reordering phrases.
*   **Formal vs. Informal:** This translation leans towards a more formal register, suitable for official documents or reports.

**Important Considerations for Low-Resource Languages:**

*   **Context is Key:**  The best translation always depends on the specific context. If you can provide more information about where this translation will be used, I can refine it further.
*   **Yoruba Dialects:** Yoruba has several dialects. This translation aims for a generally understandable form, but there might be slight variations preferred in specific regions.
*   **Review by a Native Speaker:**  Ideally, a native Yoruba speaker should review this translation to ensure accuracy and naturalness.  I am an AI and while I strive for accuracy, I cannot guarantee it without human review.



I hope this translation is helpful! Let me know if you have any other documents you'd like me to translate or if you have any questions about this one.

translations.source: Transport policies and investments that favour clean public transport, along with walking and cycling, can have a dramatic impact on air quality. This can reduce multiple health risks at the same time, including stroke, heart attack, lung disease and some cancers. They can also reduce the estimated 1.25 million deaths annually from traffic injury, while also improving access to health services, particularly in densely populated areas. Sustainable development housing policies can further reduce emissions through considerations such as building siting and land use, choices of construction materials, design features and ventilation and energy. WHO response: WHO’s work is guided by the Sustainable Development Goals, particularly Goal 3: “Ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages.”
translations.reference: Àwọn ìlànà ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àti ìdókòwò tí wọ́n ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àwùjọ mímọ́, pẹ̀lú ìrìn àti kẹ̀kẹ́ gígùn, lè ní ipa rere lórí dídára afẹ́fẹ́. Èyí lè ṣe àdínkù àwọn ewu ìlera lásìkò kan náà, tí ó fi mọ́ ẹ̀gbà, àìsàn ọkàn, àìsàn ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró àti àwọn jẹjẹrẹ kan. Wọ́n tún lè ṣe àdínkù ìṣirò àwọn ikú mílíọ̀nù 1.25 ọdọọdún láti ara ìfarapa òpópónà, nígbà tí ó ń mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá àǹfààní sí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àwọn ènìyàn pọ̀ sí. Àwọn ìlànà ilégbèé ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése lè túbọ̀ ṣe àdínkù àwọn ìtújáde nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìpinnu bí ilẹ̀ ilé àti ìlò ilẹ̀, àwọn àṣàyàn ohun èlò ìkọ́lé, àwọn àbùdá àgbékalẹ̀ àti afẹ́fẹ́ òhun agbára. Èsì WHO: Iṣẹ́ WHO ń gbà ìtọ́nisọ́nà láti ọwọ́ àwọn Àfojúsùn Ìdàgbàsókè Kánrinkése, pàápàá jùlọ Àfojúsùn 3: "Ẹ rí i dájú pé ẹ ní ìgbé ayé ìlera àti ìgbéga àlàáfíà fún gbogbo ọjọ́ orí."

translations.source: The Goals inform projects and policies across the Organization, including guidelines on housing, urban development, transportation systems and health care delivery. For example, the WHO Housing and health guidelines, first published in 2018, highlight the increasing impact of housing conditions on human health in light of urban growth, climate and demographic changes. The guidelines provide Member States, partners and the public with evidence-based recommendations relevant to inadequate living space (crowding), low and high indoor temperatures, injury hazards in the home, and accessibility of housing for people with functional impairments. The Urban Health Initiative (UHI) also focuses on ways for cities to enable good health by catalysing effective action on urban air pollution and short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)—saving lives by linking health, environment and sustainable development. WHO also collaborates with the Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC) on the Breathe Life campaign, which aims to cut air pollution and reduce the 7 million premature deaths annually that it causes.
translations.reference: Àwọn Àfojúsùn náà mú àwọn iṣẹ́ àkànṣe àti àwọn ìlànà káàkiri Àjọ wà, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ìtọ́nisọ́nà lórí ilégbèé, ìdàgbàsókè ìlú, àwọn ètò ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àti àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́jú ìlera. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn ilégbèé WHO àti ìtọ́nisọ́nà ìlera, tí wọ́n kọ́kọ́ tẹ̀ jáde ní 2018, ṣe ìdámọ̀ ipa àwọn ìṣòro ilégbèé tí ó ń pọ̀ sí i lórí ìlera ọmọnìyàn nítorí ìdàgbàsókè ìlú, ojú ọjọ́ àti àwọn àyípadà iye ènìyàn. Àwọn ìtọ́nisọ́nà ń pèsè àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́, àwọn alábàáṣiṣẹ́pọ̀ àti àwùjọ pẹ̀lú àwọn àbá adálórí-ẹ̀rí tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìtó ààyè ìgbé (ọ̀pọ̀ èèyàn), àwọn ìwọ̀n ìgbóná kékeré àti gọbọi inú ilé, àwọn ewu ìfarapa nínú ilé, àti níní àǹfààní sí ilégbèé fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní ìṣòro iṣẹ́ ṣíṣe. Ètò Ìlera Ìgboro (EII) tún fojúsun àwọn ọ̀nà fún ìlú láti fààyè gba ìlera rere nípa ṣíṣe àyàṣe iṣẹ́ tí ó múnádóko lórí ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ ìlú àti àwọn abàyíkájẹ́ ojú ọjọ́ ọlọ́jọ́ péréte (SLCPs) – gbígba ẹ̀mí là nípa mímú àjọṣepọ̀ bá ìlera, àyíká àti ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése. Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé tún ṣe àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àgbájọ Afẹ́fẹ́ àti Ojú ọjọ́ mímọ́ lórí Ìpolongo Ayé Ìmí, èyí tí ó fojúsun mímú àdínkù bá ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ àti mímú àdínkù bá mílíọ̀nù 7 ikú àìtọ́jọ́ lọ́dọọdún tí ó ń fà.

translations.source: Syphilis Overview: Syphilis is a bacterial sexually transmitted infection (STI) caused by Treponema pallidum which results in substantial morbidity and mortality, and it is curable. Syphilis is transmitted through sexual contact with the infectious lesions, via blood transfusion or from a pregnant woman to her fetus. Mother-to-child transmission of syphilis is usually devastating to the fetus in cases that were not detected or treated sufficiently early in pregnancy. Maternal syphilis, when untreated, treated late or not treated with penicillin, results in adverse birth outcomes (ABOs) estimated in 50−80% of cases, depending on the stage of syphilis.
translations.reference: Sífílì Ìsọnísókí: Sífílì jẹ́ àìsàn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ tí kòkòrò bakitéríà ń tàn ká láti ọwọ́ Treponema pallidum èyí tí ó máa ń fa ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ewu àìsàn àti ikú, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ṣe é wòsàn. Sífílì máa ń ràn ká nípasẹ̀ ìfarakínra ìbálòpọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn ògòdò àrùn, nípasẹ̀ ìfàjẹ̀sínilára tàbí láti ara aláboyún obìnrin sí ọmọ inú rẹ̀. Ìkóràn sífílì láti ara ìyá-sí-ọmọ sáábà máa ń le púpọ̀ fún ọlẹ̀ ní àwọn ọ̀ràn tí wọ́n kò dá mọ̀ tàbí ṣe ìtọ́jú tó níbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìlóyún. Sífílì ìyálọ́mọ, nígbà tí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, wọ́n pẹ́ tọ́jú rẹ̀ tàbí wọn kò tọ́jú rẹ pẹlu pẹnisilíìnì, ń yọrí sí àwọn àbájáde ìbí líle nínú ìṣirò àwọn ọ̀ràn 50-80%, dá lórí ìpele sífílì.

Okay, here's a translation of the provided English text into Yoruba, aiming for clarity and accuracy while considering the nuances of the language. I'll also include some notes afterward explaining some of the choices made.

**Yoruba Translation:**

Imọ̀ ńlá nípa Sífilisi: Sífilisi jẹ́ ìyàbá ẹlẹ́gàn ti o mu lati ọ̀rọ̀ sẹ́xù, o jẹ́ abinibi tí Treponema pallidum nímọ̀ rírọ̀, àti pé a le máa ń ṣíṣúnyà àti máa ń ku nígbà tí a ba ríráru. Sífilisi faagun nipá ìdotun àárọ̀ríkú sẹ́xù pẹ̀lú àwọn ìwọn tí ó ńkóré, nígbà tí a ba gbé ẹ̀ẹ́, tàbí níbíjú àárọ̀ríkú ìdáná.  Ìléra sífilisi tí òun tó ibi ìbàjẹ́ àárọ̀ríkú, ó máa ń ṣòro fún ọmọdé lẹ́ẹ̀, nínú àwọn ìṣẹ́ tí kò mọ́ tàbí tí kò mú ìwọ̀ àádọ̀gbò lọ́wọ́ láìsán.  Ìyàbá ẹlẹ́gàn tí òun tó ibi ìbàjẹ́ àárọ̀ríkú, tí kò ní ohun tó ṣe, tàbí tí a ba múlẹ̀ nígbà tó nìkan, tàbí tí kò ní ohun tó ṣe pẹ̀lú penicillin, máa ń mú ìwọn ìbáwọn tí a nìkan (ABOs) níbíjú àwọn ìṣẹ́ 50–80%, da lori ìgbà tí sífilisi báyìí.

**Pronunciation Guide (Approximate - Yoruba pronunciation can vary by dialect):**

*   **Sífilisi:** See-fee-lee-see
*   **ìyàbá ẹlẹ́gàn:** ee-yah-bah eh-leh-gahnn

*   **penicillin:** peh-neecil-in
*   **ABOs:** Ah-boh-s

**Notes on Translation Choices & Considerations:**

*   **Technical Terms:** I've tried to use the most appropriate Yoruba equivalents for medical terms.  "STI" is translated as "ìyàbá ẹlẹ́gàn ti o mu lati ọ̀rọ̀ sẹ́xù" (sexually transmitted disease).  "Morbidity and mortality" is translated as "a le máa ń ṣíṣúnyà àti máa ń ku" (can cause sickness and death).
*   **"Mother-to-child transmission":**  Translated as "Ìléra sífilisi tí òun tó ibi ìbàjẹ́ àárọ̀ríkú" (Syphilis disease that affects pregnancy).
*   **"Adverse birth outcomes (ABOs)":**  I kept the abbreviation "ABOs" as it is, and added a translation of what it means in parentheses: "ìwọn ìbáwọn tí a nìkan" (the rate of problems that occur).  Using the abbreviation is common in medical contexts, even in Yoruba.
*   **Sentence Structure:** I've adjusted the sentence structure to flow more naturally in Yoruba. Yoruba often uses a more indirect and descriptive style than English.
*   **Clarity:** I prioritized clarity and ensuring the meaning is easily understood by a Yoruba-speaking audience.
*   **Dialect:** Yoruba has several dialects. This translation aims for a generally understandable form, but some phrasing might be more common in certain regions than others.
*   **Context is Key:**  For a truly polished translation, it would be helpful to know the specific context in which this document will be used (e.g., a patient information leaflet, a medical training manual).

**Disclaimer:**  I am an AI and cannot provide medical advice. This translation is for informational purposes only and should be reviewed by a qualified Yoruba-speaking medical professional for accuracy and suitability for its intended use.  It's crucial to have a human expert verify any medical translation.

translations.source: These ABOs are often severe and include stillbirth (most commonly), neonatal death, prematurity, low birth weight, and congenitally infected infants. Syphilis can be prevented through safer sex practices including the correct and consistent use of condoms. Many people with syphilis do not have any symptoms or have only minor symptoms that go unnoticed. Identifying infection through laboratory tests and treatment of positive cases will prevent further transmission and adverse pregnancy outcomes including congenital syphilis. Screening can be done using rapid tests that can provide results in less than 20 minutes, allowing for immediate treatment.
translations.reference: Àwọn àbájáde ìbí líle sáábà máa ń tí ó fi mọ́ àbíkú (lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà), ikú ìkókó, àìgbó, ìwọ̀n ìwúwo ìbí pínnísín, àti àwọn ìkókó tí àìsàn sọ di abiùn. Sífílì lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ àwọn àṣà ìbálòpọ̀ aláàbò tí ó fi mọ́ ìlò ọ̀rá ìdáàbòbò bí ó ti tọ́ àti déédéé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú sífílì kò ní èyíkéyìí àmì àìsàn kankan tàbí ní àwọn àmì àìsàn kékeré tí wọ́n kì í ṣàkíyèsí. Ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ àìsàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ọ̀ràn àìsàn yóò dènà ìkóràn síwájú àti àbájáde ìlóyún líle tí ó fi mọ́ àbájáde àrùn sífílì. Àyẹ̀wò lè ṣe é ṣe nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn àyẹ̀wò ayákánkán tí wọ́n lè pèsè àbájáde ní kò tó ìṣẹ́jú 20, tí ó ń fi ààyè gba ìtọ́jú àsìkò.

**Notes on Translation Choices:**

*   **ABO:** I've kept "ABO" as is, assuming it's a specific medical term that might be recognized.  If a Yoruba equivalent is known and commonly used, it could be substituted.

*   **Neonatal Death:** "Iku ọmọdé tí ó ṣiṣe" translates to "death of a child that just happened/was born."
*   **Prematurity:** "Ìgbà tí ó bẹ́ẹ̀" means "the time of being born early."

*   **Congenitally Infected Infants:** "Awọn ọmọdé tí a bẹ́ẹ̀ wọ́n ní ìṣeju" translates to "children who were born with syphilis."
*   **Safer Sex Practices:** "Ṣiṣiṣi ohun èlẹ́yìn tí ó tọ" means "using condoms correctly."
*   **Laboratory Tests:** "Àwọn idanití ilẹ̀ẹ́kọ̀ọ́" translates to "scientific tests."
*   **Rapid Tests:** "Àwọn idanití tí ó le pèsè àwọn abajọtí ní ìgbẹ̀rin ọ̀rọ̀ tí kéré ju 20 mẹ́rin lọ" is a longer phrase meaning "tests that can give results in less than 20 minutes."  It's more descriptive to ensure understanding.
*   **Immediate Treatment:** "Itọju lẹ́nu" means "quick treatment."

**Important Considerations:**

*   **Dialect:** Yoruba has different dialects. This translation aims for a generally understandable form, but regional variations may exist.
*   **Context:**  The best translation always depends on the specific context and target audience.
*   **Review by a Native Speaker:**  It is *highly recommended* that a native Yoruba speaker, preferably one with medical knowledge, review this translation for accuracy and cultural appropriateness before use.

translations.source: When untreated, syphilis lasts many years and goes through different stages. Treatment for syphilis is done with injectable penicillin benzathine, with 1 to 3 doses depending on the stage of the disease. Only penicillin can prevent congenital syphilis. Treatment with penicillin has been hampered by periodic and prolonged shortages of benzathine penicillin in numerous countries, including high-, low- and middle-income countries. Symptoms: Many people with syphilis do not present symptoms or do not perceive them.
translations.reference: Nígbà tí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, sífílì yóò lọ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò la onírúurú ìpele kọjá. Ìtọ́jú fún sífílì máa ń jẹ́ ṣíṣe pẹ̀lú òògùn pẹnisilíìnì benzathine tí ó ṣe é gún bí abẹ́rẹ́, pẹ̀lú ìlò 1 sí 3 èyí tí ó dá lórí ìpele àìsàn náà. Pẹnisilíìnì nìkan ló lè dènà àwọn sífílì àsìkò ìbí. Ìtọ́jú pẹ̀lú pẹnisilíìnì ti ní ìdènà láti ọwọ́ ọ̀wọ́ngógó òòrèkóòrè àti ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ ní àwọn onírúurú orílẹ̀ èdè, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi, pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú sífílì kì í ṣàfihàn àwọn àmì àìsàn tàbí wọn kì í ní ìmọ̀lára wọn.

Itọju fún ṣíṣu jẹ́ nípa fifọ́ penicillin benzathine sínú àwọn èṣẹ, pẹ̀lú àwọn ìdòsì kan dé ọ̀tún mẹ́ta, gùn ju ìgbà àìní ti àwọn ìṣe. Penicillin nìkan tí ó le ṣe ìranan ṣíṣu tí ó ń ṣiṣe fún ọmọdé. translations.source: Untreated, syphilis lasts many years and is characterized into different stages. Sexual transmission typically occurs during early stages, i.e., up to 2 years after infection. During the initial phase (primary syphilis), which lasts on average 21 days, a solitary, painless, usually hard and round sore (chancre) appears at the site of inoculation (contact), often in the vagina, penis or anus (but it may also be extra-genital) and may go unnoticed. The chancre heals with or without treatment within 3 to 10 weeks. If untreated, the disease progresses to the secondary stage.
translations.reference: Sífílì, àìtọ́jú lo ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó pín sí onírúurú àwọn ìpele:. Ìkóràn ìbálòpọ̀ máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lásìkò ìbẹ̀rẹ̀pẹ̀pẹ̀, ìyẹn ni pé, fún bí ọdún méjì lẹ́yìn àrùn. Lásìkò ìpele ìsáájú (sífílì ìpìlẹ̀), èyí tí yóò lo bí ọjọ́ 21, egbò àdáwà, aláìnírora roboto tí ó sáábà máa ń le kan (egbò) yóò farahàn ní agbègbè ìgbàsára (ìfarakanra), ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà nínú òbò, okó tàbí fùrọ̀ (ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè jẹ́ àfikún ojú ara) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè lọ láìfarahàn. Ògòdò náà yóò gba ìwòsàn pẹ̀lú tàbí láìsí ìwòsàn láàrin ọ̀sẹ̀ 3 tàbí 10. Bí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, àìsàn náà yóò tẹ̀síwájú sí ìpele àtẹ̀lé.

translations.source: Secondary syphilis is characterized by a rash that can vary widely and mimic other infectious or non-infectious conditions, but characteristically affects the palms and soles. The rash is non-itchy and can be minimal enough to be overlooked. In warm and moist areas of the body, such as the anus and labia, large white or grey raised lesions may develop at the location of the primary chancre (condyloma lata). The symptoms and signs of secondary syphilis spontaneously resolve, even without treatment. Without treatment, the latent stage starts.
translations.reference: Sífílì àtẹ̀lé ní máa ń ní àbùdá kúrúkùsá tí ó lè jẹ́ onírúurú kí ó sì sé ìsìnjẹ àwọn ìṣòro àrùn tàbí àìsàn mìíràn, ṣùgbọ́n ó sáábà máa ń mú àtẹ́lẹwọ́ àti àtẹ̀lesẹ̀. Kúrúkùsá náà kì í húnni bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè kéré láti jẹ́ àfojúfò. Ní àwọn ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n gbóná tí wọ́n sì ní omi, bí ihò ìdí àti ẹnu, ògòdò títóbi funfun tàbí ewú lè dàgbà ní ọ̀gangan egbò ìpìlẹ̀ (condyloma lata). Àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti àmì sífílì àtẹ̀lé máa ń sàn fúnra rẹ̀, kódà láìsí ìtọ́jú. Láìsí ìtọ́jú, ìpele afarasin bẹ̀rẹ̀.

translations.source: Latent syphilis presents no clinical symptoms or signs and the treatment is different from the early phase. After years or decades without treatment, neurological and cardiovascular diseases among others may occur. Treatment: Syphilis diagnosis is based on the person’s history, physical examination, laboratory testing and sometimes radiology, as symptoms are not common or noticeable. Identifying asymptomatic infection through laboratory tests and treatment of positive cases will prevent further transmission and adverse pregnancy outcomes, including congenital syphilis. Laboratory tests for syphilis include direct detection of T. pallidum or indirect methods such as serology.
translations.reference: Sífílì afarasin kì í ṣàfihàn àwọn àmì àìsàn àti àpẹẹrẹ kankan bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìtọ́jú rẹ̀ yàtọ̀ sí ìpele ìbẹ̀rẹ̀. Lẹ́yìn àwọn ọdún tàbí ẹ̀wádún láìsí ìtọ́jú, àwọn àìsàn ajẹmọ́ ọ̀páyìnmọ́pọlọ àti àwọn àìsàn ọkàn àti àwọn mìíràn lè wáyé. Ìtọ́jú: Àyẹ̀wò ìtọ́jú sífílì máa ń dá lórí ìtàn, àyẹ̀wò ojúkojú, àyẹ̀wò láàbù ènìyàn àti nígbà mìíràn àyẹ̀wò ẹ̀rọ afẹ́wẹ́lẹ́, nítorí àmì àìsàn kò wọ́pọ̀ tàbí farahàn. Ìdámọ̀ àrùn aláìlámì àìsàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ọ̀ràn àrùn yóò dènà ìkóràn síwájú sí i àti àwọn àbájáde oyún líle, tí ó fi mọ́ sífílì amútinúwá. Àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù fún sífílì tí ó fi mọ́ ìdámọ̀ tààrà T. pallidum tàbí ìdámọ̀ tààrà bí àyẹ̀wò sẹ́rọ́ọ̀mù inú ẹ̀jẹ̀.

**Yoruba Translation:**

Irukuru sífilisi ko ni àwọn àfihanrara titi di ami eruku àti àṣà, àti pé àfiọnṣẹpệlẹ́ rírọ̀ tọ́ cán dé ohun tí ó yẹ kí a ṣe nìbẹ̀ tí ó yatọ̀ sí ìgbà ìmọ̀ àti ìrọ̀. Lẹ́yìn ọdún àwọn ọ̀rọ̀ tàbí àwọn ìgbà díẹ̀ tí kò sí àfiọnṣẹpệlẹ́, àwọn àmúlẹ́ tí ó ṣe pọ̀ sí àjùmọ̀ àti awọn ilẹ̀ èkúnrẹ́, lára àwọn èmi-mije gbogbo, yáa ó máa ń ṣiṣẹ́. Àfiọnṣẹpệlẹ́: Àwọn ìwífún ìwà sífilisi fún ìran lórí itan ènìyàn, ìtàn-ìnú ènìyàn, àwọn àwọn ìdánimọ̀ àwọn ìṣe, àti bí ó ti ṣe dé, àwọn àwọn àkànṣọ̀ṣọ̀, nígbà tí àwọn àfihanrara kò wà láti jẹ́ òwúrọ̀ tàbí ọ̀wọ́. Ìdánimọ̀ àfiọnṣẹpệlẹ́ ìyàtọ̀ ní àwọn ìdánimọ̀ àwọn ìṣe àti àfiọnṣẹpệlẹ́ àwọn ìwà tí ó ba jẹ́, yóò da bọ́ àfiànán àti àwọn ìgbà tí a ò ní yíò láti ṣiṣi, pẹ̀lú sífilisi tí ó wà nínú ìyàwó. Àwọn ìdánimọ̀ àwọn ìṣe fún sífilisi pẹlú àwọn ìdánimọ̀ ìwà tó tóótọ̀ ti T. pallidum tàbí àwọn ọ̀na àfiọnṣẹpệlẹ́ tí ó jẹ́ àfiọnṣẹpệlẹ́.

**Notes on Translation Choices & Explanations:**

*   **"Latent syphilis"**: Translated as "Irukuru sífilisi" (Hidden syphilis). "Irukuru" conveys the idea of something being latent or hidden.
*   **"Clinical symptoms or signs"**: Translated as "àwọn àfihanrara titi di ami eruku àti àṣà" (Symptoms and signs up to the point of disease). This is a common way to express this in Yoruba medical contexts.
*   **"Treatment is different from the early phase"**: Translated as "àfiọnṣẹpệlẹ́ rírọ̀ tọ́ cán dé ohun tí ó yẹ kí a ṣe nìbẹ̀ tí ó yatọ̀ sí ìgbà ìmọ̀ àti ìrọ̀" (Treatment is what should be done there that is different from the early stage and symptoms).
*   **"Neurological and cardiovascular diseases"**: Translated as "àwọn àmúlẹ́ tí ó ṣe pọ̀ sí àjùmọ̀ àti awọn ilẹ̀ èkúnrẹ́" (Diseases that affect the brain and heart).
*   **"Diagnosis is based on..."**: Translated as "Àwọn ìwífún ìwà sífilisi fún ìran lórí..." (Syphilis diagnosis is based on...).
*   **"Asymptomatic infection"**: Translated as "àfiọnṣẹpệlẹ́ ìyàtọ̀" (Different treatment).
*   **"Congenital syphilis"**: Translated as "sífilisi tí ó wà nínú ìyàwó" (Syphilis that is in pregnancy).
*   **"Serology"**: Translated as "àwọn ọ̀na àfiọnṣẹpệlẹ́ tí ó jẹ́ àfiọnṣẹpệlẹ́" (Methods that are treatment).

**Important Considerations for Low-Resource Languages:**

*   **Medical Terminology:**  Yoruba medical terminology is still developing. I've used the most appropriate terms I could find, but some terms might need further refinement or clarification with a Yoruba-speaking medical professional.
*   **Context is Key:**  The best translation always depends on the specific context. If this document is for a particular audience (e.g., healthcare providers, general public), the language might need to be adjusted accordingly.
*   **Review by a Native Speaker:**  It's *crucial* to have this translation reviewed by a native Yoruba speaker, ideally someone with medical knowledge, to ensure accuracy and naturalness.

translations.source: Rapid screening and diagnostic tests are also available and can provide results in less than 20 minutes, facilitating immediate treatment initiation. WHO recommends syphilis to be treated with injectable penicillin (benzathine penicillin G) with 1 or 3 doses, depending on the stage of the disease. WHO has detailed treatment guidelines for syphilis, and one specifically for the screening and treatment of syphilis in pregnancy. Congenital syphilis There are currently no diagnostic tests for congenital syphilis.
translations.reference: Àwọn àgbéyẹ̀wò àti àyẹ̀wò kánkán tún wà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè pèsè àwọn àbájáde ní kò tó ìṣẹ́jú 20, tí yóò sì mú ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìtọ́jú kánkán wá. Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé dábàá pé kí sífílì máa jẹ́ títọ́jú nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò abẹ́rẹ́ pẹnisilíìnì (benzathine penicillin G) pẹ̀lú ìlò 1 tàbí mẹ́ta, dá lórí ìpele àìsàn náà. WHO ti ṣàlàyé àwọn ìlànà ìtọ́jú fún sífílì, àti ọkàn pàtàkì fún àyẹ̀wò àti ìtọ́jú sífílì nínú oyún. Sífílì amútinúwá Kò sí àyẹ̀wò kánkán báyìí fún àwọn sífílì amútinúwá.

translations.source: All live or stillborn infants of women with syphilis should be examined for evidence of congenital syphilis. Live-born infants should be examined and tested at birth to define treatment requirement. When indicated, also at monthly intervals for 3 months until the infant’s serology are, and remain, negative, as maternal antibodies can be passively transmitted to the baby during pregnancy.
translations.reference: Gbogbo alààyè tàbí àbíkú ìkókó àwọn obìnrin tí wọ́n ní sífílì gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ àgbéyẹ̀wò fún ẹ̀rí sífílì amútinúwá. Àwọn ìkókó àbíyè gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ yíyẹ̀wò àti àyẹ̀wò lásìkò ìbí láti sọ òté ìtọ́jú. Nígbà tí wọ́n bá tọ́ka sí i, bákan náà ní fún àlàfo oṣooṣù fún oṣù 3 títí àyẹ̀wò sẹ́rọ́ọ̀mù inú ẹ̀jẹ̀ ìkókó, tí ó sì wà, láìsí àmì àìsàn, nítorí àwọn àjẹsára ìyá lè ran ọmọ náà lásìkò oyún láìṣeṣẹ́.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's the Yoruba translation of the provided text, aiming for accuracy and clarity while considering the nuances of the language. I'll also include some notes afterward explaining some of the choices made.

**Yoruba Translation:**

Ọmọ-ọkùnrin tí ó ní àgbàtàbẹ́ẹ́, tí ó níbe láàárín àwọn tí ó wà ní ìgbà tí ó ní àgbàtàbẹ́ẹ́, tàbí àwọn tí ó kò sí ní ìgbà náà, gbọ́yá tún wo fún ìwífúnánìí nípa àgbàtàbẹ́ẹ́ tí ó yà lẹ́ẹ̀. Àwọn ọmọ-ọkùnrin tí ó wà ní ìgbà náà gbọ́yá wo àti ṣe àwọn idanihun nígbà tí ó ní àgbàtàbẹ́ẹ́ láti mọ̀ pé kí tó ṣe ohun tó yẹ. Nígbà tó ṣe dé, tun wo ni àwọn ìgbà óṣù kan kan fún àwọn óṣù méjì díẹ̀dé, títí ìfọ̀rọ̀wé ọmọdé náà, ó sì máa ń ṣí, bí ó ti ṣe patịpá àgbàtàbẹ́ẹ́ ìyàwó, nitori pé àwọn àgbàtàbẹ́ẹ́ ìyàwó le ṣe afihan lára ara ọmọdé tí ó ń gbé lẹ́ẹ̀.

**Pronunciation Guide (approximate - Yoruba pronunciation can vary by dialect):**

*   **Ọmọ-ọkùnrin:** Oh-moh oh-koon-reen
*   **Tí ó ní àgbàtàbẹ́ẹ́:** Tee oh nee ah-gbah-tah-beh-eh
*   **Tàbí:** Tah-bee
*   **Àwọn:** Ah-won
*   **Tí ó kò sí:** Tee oh koh see
*   **Ìgbà náà:** Ee-gbah nah
*   **Gbọ́yá tún wo:** Goh-yah toon woh
*   **Ìwífúnánìí:** Ee-wee-foo-nah-nee
*   **Nípa:** Nee-pah

*   **Bí ó ti ṣe patịpá àgbàtàbẹ́ẹ́ ìyàwó:** Bee oh tee seh pah-tee-pah ah-gbah-tah-beh-eh ee-yah-wo
*   **Nitori pé:** Nee-toh-ree peh
*   **Àwọn àgbàtàbẹ́ẹ́ ìyàwó:** Ah-won ah-gbah-tah-beh-eh ee-yah-wo
*   **Le ṣe afihan lára ara ọmọdé tí ó ń gbé lẹ́ẹ̀:** Leh seh ah-fee-hahn lah-rah ah-rah oh-moh-deh tee oh neh geh leh-eh

**Notes on Translation Choices:**

*   **"Syphilis":**  I used "àgbàtàbẹ́ẹ́" which is the standard Yoruba term for syphilis.
*   **"Congenital Syphilis":** "Ìwífúnánìí nípa àgbàtàbẹ́ẹ́ tí ó yà lẹ́ẹ̀" - This translates to "the diagnosis of syphilis that is born with." It's a descriptive way to convey the meaning.
*   **"Live-born infants":** "Àwọn ọmọ-ọkùnrin tí ó wà ní ìgbà tí ó ní àgbàtàbẹ́ẹ́" - "The children who were present at the time of syphilis."
*   **"Stillborn infants":** "Àwọn tí ó kò sí ní ìgbà náà" - "Those who were not present at that time."
*   **"Treatment requirement":** "Kí tó ṣe ohun tó yẹ" - "What is appropriate to do."
*   **"Maternal antibodies":** "Àgbàtàbẹ́ẹ́ ìyàwó" - "The syphilis of the wife/mother."
*   **"Passively transmitted":** "Le ṣe afihan lára ara ọmọdé" - "Can be shown within the body of the child."
*   **Sentence Structure:** I've adjusted the sentence structure to flow more naturally in Yoruba.  Direct word-for-word translations often sound awkward.

*   **Context is Key:**  The best translation always depends on the specific context. If this is for a patient education leaflet, the language might need to be simplified further.

translations.source: Taeniasis and cysticercosis Overview: Infection with the tapeworm Taenia solium can result in two distinct conditions: taeniasis and cysticercosis. Taeniasis is the intestinal infection with the tapeworm and it occurs when a person eats raw or undercooked, infected pork. Taeniasis has no major impact on human health. However, tapeworm eggs pass in the faeces of the tapeworm carrier and in the absence of a proper sanitation may contaminate the environment.
translations.reference: Taeníásí àti sisikasósì Ìsọnísókí: Àìsàn pẹ̀lú aràn pẹlẹbẹ atara-ẹrankosẹ̀ lè fa àwọn ìṣòro ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ méjì: àrùn taẹniásì àti sisikakósì. Taeníásí jẹ́ àrùn ìfun pẹ̀lú aràn pẹlẹbẹ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó máa ń wáyé nígbà tí ènìyàn bá jẹ ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ tútù tàbí tí kò jinná, tàbí ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ tí ó ní àìsàn. Taeníásí kò ní ipa gbòógì kankan lórí ìlera ẹ̀dá. Àmọ́ sá, àwọn ẹyin aràn pẹlẹbẹ tí ẹni tí ó ní aràn pẹlẹbẹ nínú yóò jáde bọ́ síta lára ìgbẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àìsí ìmọ́tótó tí ó tọ́ lè mú àyíká di àìmọ́.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's the Yoruba translation of the provided text, aiming for clarity and accuracy while considering the nuances of the language. I'll also include some notes afterward explaining some of the choices made.

**Yoruba Translation:**

Iwe-iwadi Taeniasis ati Cysticercosis: Iṣoṣi pẹpẹẹ Taenia solium le mu awọn ipinlẹ mejeji kan ṣi: taeniasis ati cysticercosis. Taeniasis ni iṣoṣi ti ara igba pẹpẹẹ, o si ṣi nigba ti ènìyàn jẹ ọgarịa ti ko ti ṣi daradara tabi ti ko ti kún rírù, tó ní iṣoṣi. Taeniasis ko ni ìmọ̀ pọ̀ nípa àpilẹ́ àgbàdọ̀. Sibẹ́darí, awọn obìnú pẹpẹẹẹ wọlé nínú èdè ti alàárìn pẹpẹẹẹ, àti bí ko ba sí ìṣàkóso èdè tí ó dara, ó máa ń fi ṣi ìdánù.

**Pronunciation Guide (approximate - Yoruba pronunciation can vary by dialect):**

*   **Iwe-iwadi:** Eee-weh-ee-wah-dee
*   **Taeniasis:** Tay-nee-ah-sees
*   **Cysticercosis:** Sis-tee-ker-koh-sees

translations.source: While tapeworm eggs are infective for pigs, T. solium eggs may also infect humans if they are ingested by a person (via the fecal-oral route, or by ingesting contaminated food or water). Ingestion of tapeworm eggs causes infection with the larval parasite in the tissues such as muscles, skin, eyes and the central nervous system (human cysticercosis), with possible devastating effects on health. When cysts develop in the brain, the condition is referred to as neurocysticercosis. Symptoms: Taeniasis is usually characterized by mild and non-specific symptoms associated to an intestinal infection (abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhoea, etc.). These symptoms may continue until the tapeworm dies following treatment, otherwise it may live for years.
translations.reference: Nígbà tí aràn pẹlẹbẹ lè mú ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ ṣàìsàn, ẹyin T.solium náà lè mú kí ènìyàn ṣàìsàn bí ènìyàn bá jẹ wọn (nípasẹ̀ ọ̀nà ìgbẹ́-sí-ẹnu, tàbí nípa jíjẹ oúnjẹ tàbí omi tí ó ti di àìmọ́). Jíjẹ ẹyin aràn pẹlẹbẹ máa ń fa àìsàn pẹ̀lú ajọ̀fẹ́ bí ìdin nínú àwọn ìṣù ara bí isan, awọ ara, ojú àti ètò òpónà ọpọlọmẹ́yìn àárín gbùngbùn (sisikakósì ènìyàn), pẹ̀lú ipá líle lórí ìlera. Nígbà tí àwọn ògòdò paali bá dàgbà nínú ọpọlọ, àìsàn náà ni wọ́n ń pè ní nirosisikakósì. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Taeníásí sáábà máa ń ní àbùdá abọ́ọ́dé àti àwọn àbùdá aláìsọpàtó tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìsàn ìfun (inú rírun, àyà rínrìn, ìgbẹ́ gbuuru, abbl.). Àwọn àmì àìsàn wọ̀nyí lè tẹ̀síwájú títí aràn pẹlẹbẹ náà yóò fi kú lẹ́yìn ìtọ́jú, bí bẹ́ẹ̀ kọ́ ó lè wà láyé fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún.

**Yoruba Translation:**

Ibi awọn ara igba (tapeworm) jẹ́ àfiànfẹ́ fún àwọn ẹranko, awọn ara igba T. solium sì máa ń le òun rírù àwọn ènìyàn nìbá ti wọ́n bá gbá wọn lẹ́nu (lẹ́nu tí ó ti wà lábẹ́ ìdán, tàbí níbá ti wọ́n bá gbá àwọn àṣe àfiànfẹ́ jùrọ̀ tàbí oríṣiríṣi). Ìgbà tí àwọn ara igba wọ jẹ́ àfiànfẹ́, yíò mu kí àwọn ènìyàn rí i àfiànfẹ́ nínú àwọn apata, gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn àramọdún, oúnjẹ, ògo, àti ìbejìrì (cysticercosis àwọn ènìyàn), pẹ̀lú àwọn ìràwọn tó le mú ìpọ̀njú àti àìsálá ti àgbàdo. Nígbà tí àwọn àfiànfẹ́ wọ yá sínú oríṣiríṣi, a máa ń tọ̀ótọ̀ yí ó nì neurocysticercosis. Àwọn ìwífún: Taeniasis jẹ́ òwúrọ̀ ní àwọn ìwífún tí ó jẹ́ èsìn àti àìgbélárí tó ní àmi àfiànfẹ́ nínú àpótí (ẹ̀rù abọ́, ìgbàgbé, ìgbàlálá, ìtààn, ìgbàgbé, àádí). Wọ̀npá àwọn ìwífún wọ̀npá tó ju sẹ́dé tí àwọn ara igba yóò kú àfi àfiànfẹ́, àmígbàti ó máa ń le ń ṣiṣẹ́ fún ọdún àwọn ọdún.

**Notes on Translation Choices & Explanations:**

*   **"Tapeworm"**: I used "àwọn ara igba" which is the standard Yoruba term for tapeworm.
*   **"Infective"**: Translated as "àfiànfẹ́" which means "infectious" or "capable of causing infection."
*   **"Ingested"**: Translated as "gbá...lẹ́nu" which means "to swallow" or "to take into the mouth."
*   **"Fecal-oral route"**: Translated as "lẹ́nu tí ó ti wà lábẹ́ ìdán" - literally "mouth that has been under feces." This is a common way to express this concept in Yoruba.
*   **"Contaminated"**: Translated as "àfiànfẹ́ jùrọ̀" - "infectious/contaminated water."
*   **"Larval parasite"**: Translated as "àfiànfẹ́" (again, the concept of infection applies here).
*   **"Tissues such as muscles, skin, eyes..."**:  I used "apata, gẹ́gẹ́ bí..." which means "tissues, such as..."
*   **"Human cysticercosis"**: I kept "cysticercosis" as it's a technical term and doesn't have a direct Yoruba equivalent. I added "àwọn ènìyàn" (human) for clarity.
*   **"Devastating effects"**: Translated as "ìràwọn tó le mú ìpọ̀njú àti àìsálá ti àgbàdo" - "effects that can cause suffering and destruction of the body."
*   **"Neurocysticercosis"**:  Similar to cysticercosis, I kept the term and added "oríṣiríṣi" (brain) for clarity.
*   **"Symptoms"**: Translated as "Àwọn ìwífún" which means "signs/symptoms."
*   **"Taeniasis"**: I kept "Taeniasis" as it's a technical term.
*   **"Mild and non-specific"**: Translated as "èsìn àti àìgbélárí" - "gentle/mild and unclear/unspecific."
*   **"Intestinal infection"**: Translated as "àfiànfẹ́ nínú àpótí" - "infection in the stomach/intestine."
*   **"Otherwise it may live for years"**: Translated as "àmígbàti ó máa ń le ń ṣiṣẹ́ fún ọdún àwọn ọdún" - "otherwise, it can continue to live for many years."

**Important Considerations for Low-Resource Languages:**

*   **Technical Terminology:**  As you can see, I've retained some English technical terms (cysticercosis, taeniasis) because direct Yoruba equivalents are often lacking or would be cumbersome.  It's common practice in low-resource language translation to borrow or adapt terms when necessary.
*   **Clarity over Literal Translation:** I prioritized conveying the *meaning* of the text in a way that would be easily understood by a Yoruba speaker, even if it meant deviating slightly from a word-for-word translation.
*   **Context is Key:**  The best translation always depends on the specific context and the intended audience.

translations.source: For cysticercosis, the incubation period is variable, and infected people may remain asymptomatic for years. In some endemic regions, infected people may develop visible or palpable nodules (a small solid bump or node which is solid that can be detected by touch) beneath the skin. Neurocysticercosis is associated with a variety of signs and symptoms depending on the number, size, stage and location of the pathological changes as well as the host’s immune response and the parasite’s genotype, but it can also be clinically asymptomatic. Symptoms may include chronic headaches, blindness, seizures (epilepsy if they are recurrent), hydrocephalus, meningitis, dementia and symptoms caused by lesions occupying spaces of the central nervous system. Treatment: The treatment of taeniasis by Taenia solium is important to prevent neurocysticercosis and as a tool to assist in controlling or stopping the parasite transmission cycle.
translations.reference: Fún sisikakósì, àsìkò ìsàba rẹ̀ máa ń yàtọ̀, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn tí wọ́n ní àìsàn náà lè máa ní àmì àìsàn kankan fún ọ̀pọ̀ ọdún. Ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àrùn yìí tí wọ́pọ̀, àwọn tí wọ́n ń ṣe àìsàn yìí lè ní àwọn ògòdò tí ó hànde tàbí ńlá (kókó tàbí ògòdò kékeré líle) tí ó wà nísàlẹ̀ awọ. Nirosisikakósì ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn onírúurú àmì àti àpẹẹrẹ tí ó dá lórí iye, ìtóbi, ìpele àti ibi àwọn àyípadà ara àti èsì àjẹsára aláìsàn àti àbùdá ajọ̀fẹ́ náà pẹ̀lú, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè má ní àmì àìsàn ìlera kankan. Àwọn àmì àìsàn lè ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn orí fífọ́ líle, ẹ̀fọ́jú, gìrì (wárápá bí wọ́n ba jẹ́ òòrèkóòrè), ọta orí, yínrùnyínrùn, ìgbàgbé àti àwọn àmì àìsàn tí àwọn ògòdò tí wọ́n gba àwọn ààyè ètò àkóso ibùdó ara ń fà. Ìtọ́jú: Ìtọ́jú taeníásí láti ọwọ́ Taenia solium ṣe pàtàkì láti dènà nirosisikakósì àti gẹ́gẹ́ bí irinṣẹ́ láti ṣàkóso tàbí dènà ìtànká ajọ̀fẹ́.

translations.source: The treatment can be done on an individual bases, or as Mass Drug Administration depending on the local circumstances and the control approaches being implemented. Taeniasis can be treated using anthelmintics (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). In neurocysticercosis, since the destruction of cysts may lead to an inflammatory response, specialised treatment of active disease is required and may include long courses with high doses of anthelmintic, as well as supporting therapy with corticosteroids and/or anti-epileptic drugs, and possibly surgery. The dosage and the duration of treatment can vary greatly and depend mainly on the number, size, location and developmental stage of the cysts, their surrounding inflammatory edema, acuteness and severity of clinical symptoms or signs.
translations.reference: Ìtọ́jú lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe lára ẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan, tàbí Àkóso Òògùn gbogbogbò èyí tí ó dá lórí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ abẹ́lé àti àwọn ìlànà ìṣàkóso tí wọ́n ń ṣàmúlò. Taeníásí lè jẹ́ títọ́jú nípa lílo àwọn òògùn agbógunti-ajọ̀fẹ́ (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). Nínú nirosisikakósì, níwọ̀n ìgbà tí bíba àwọn ògòdò líle jẹ́ lè fa èsì ìrẹ̀dòdò, ìtọ́jú pàtàkì àìsàn tí ó ń ṣọṣẹ́ nílò bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn iṣẹ́ ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn ìlò òògùn agbógunti-ajọ̀fẹ́, àti ìwòsàn alátìlẹ́yìn pẹ̀lú corticosteroids àti/tàbí òògùn agbógunti-wárápá, àti bóyá iṣẹ́ abẹ. Ìlò àti àsìkò ìtọ́jú lè yàtọ̀ dáadáa bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè dá lórí iye, ìwọ̀n, agbègbè, àti ìpele ìdàgbàsókè oyún líle, ìrẹ̀dòdò àyíká ọṣẹ́, ìṣòro àti bí àwọn àmì àìsàn tàbí àmì ṣe lè tó.

Okay, here's the Yoruba translation of the provided English text, aiming for accuracy and clarity while considering the nuances of the language. I'll also include some notes afterward explaining some of the choices made.

**Yoruba Translation:**

Imuṣiṣu le ṣe lilo ni ojú ìdán individual, tabi gẹgẹ bi Ilọsiwaju Ohunèlẹ ti Àwọn Dọ̀sī (Mass Drug Administration) da lori awoṣe ibi-lẹ ati awọn irọ̀rin ọna ti o n fi ṣiṣẹ. Taeniasis le ṣe imuṣiṣu pẹ̀lú àwọn àmúgbélé (anthelmintics) (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). Ní Neurocysticercosis, nitori pé ìyan oju àwọn cysts lo mú ìdánimọ̀ ṣiṣẹ, imuṣiṣu ti àwọn àfihan ilẹ́rẹ̀ lọ́wò lójúmọ̀ níwájú, ó máa ń pẹlú àwọn ìgbà tó ní ìpọ̀nnú tó ga ti àwọn àmúgbélé, àti àfikun ìṣòro pẹ̀lú àwọn corticosteroids àti/tabi àwọn ọgbin ti o gbé ìdọ̀tàn.  Ọ̀nú àti àwọn èèkàn ti imuṣiṣu le yatọ̀ pípẹ́ púpọ̀, àti pé wọ́n bẹ̀rẹ̀ púpọ̀ lórí nìkan àwọn àwọn, ìwọn, ibi, àti igba ti àwọn cysts, ìdánimọ̀ tí ó n ṣókù àwọn cysts, àti bí o ṣe nìtan àti àwọn ìwọn àmi ìlẹ́rẹ̀ tàbí àmi.

**Notes on Translation Choices:**

*   **Mass Drug Administration:** I used "Ilọsiwaju Ohunèlẹ ti Àwọn Dọ̀sī" which is a direct translation.  It's a relatively new concept in Yoruba, so a more descriptive phrase might be needed depending on the audience.
*   **Anthelmintics:**  "Àmúgbélé" is the standard Yoruba term for these medications.
*   **Neurocysticercosis:**  I've transliterated this term as it's a specific medical term.  Trying to translate it directly would likely be confusing.

*   **"Depending on the local circumstances":**  I translated this as "da lori awoṣe ibi-lẹ" which means "based on the nature of the place/location."

translations.source: Tetanus Overview: Tetanus is a serious illness contracted through exposure to the spores of the bacterium, Clostridium tetani, which live in soil, saliva, dust and manure. The bacteria can enter the body through a deep cuts, wounds or burns affecting the nervous system. The infection leads to painful muscle contractions, particularly of the jaw and neck muscle, and is commonly known as “lockjaw”. People of all ages can get tetanus but the disease is particularly common and serious in newborn babies and their mothers when the mother is unprotected from tetanus by the vaccine, tetanus toxoid.
translations.reference: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì Ìsọnísókí: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì jẹ́ àìsàn líle tí ènìyàn máa ń kó láti ara ìfaragbá ọmọ àwọn bakitéríà, Clostridium tetani, èyí tí ó ń gbé nínú ilẹ̀, itọ́, eruku àti ìgbẹ́. Bakitéríà náà lè wọ inú ara nípasẹ̀ egbò ọgbẹ́ jínjìn, àwọn ìfarapa tàbí àpá iná tí wọ́n ń nípa lórí ètò àkóso ara. Àìsàn náà máa ń yọrí sí àwọn ìsúnkì isan ara, pàápàá jùlọ àgbọ̀n àti isan ọrùn, beet sì ni s mọ̀ ọ́n sí "ìwayínpọ̀". Gbogbo àwọn ènìyàn pátá lè ní tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ṣùgbọ́n àìsàn náà wọ́pọ̀ ó sì lè láàrin àwọn ọmọ tuntun àti àwọn ìyá wọn nígbà tí ìyá náà kò bá ní ààbò láti ara tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì nípasẹ̀ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára, tetanus toxoid.

Bakteriya le wọlé inú àwọn èèkún, àwọn inú bí-ẹ̀wẹ̀, tàbí àwọn ìtẹ́wọ́ tó ń ṣíṣe lára àwọn ẹ̀sìn. translations.source: Tetanus occurring during pregnancy or within 6 weeks of the end of pregnancy is called maternal tetanus, while tetanus occurring within the first 28 days of life is called neonatal tetanus. The disease remains an important public health problem in many parts of the world, but especially in low-income countries or districts, where immunization coverage is low and unclean birth practices are common. WHO estimates that in 2018 (the latest year for which estimates are available), 25 000 newborns died from neonatal tetanus, 88% reduction from the situation in 2000. Symptoms: Nearly all cases of tetanus occur in people who have never been vaccinated or in adults who have not kept up to date on their booster shots. Signs and symptoms of tetanus appear anytime from 3 to 21 days after tetanus bacteria enter the body through a wound.
translations.reference: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì tí ó ń farahàn lásìkò oyún tàbí láàrin ọ̀sẹ̀ 6 ní ìgbẹ̀yìn oyún náà ni a ń pè ní tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìyálọ́mọ, nígbà tí tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì tí ó farahàn láàrin ọjọ́ 28 lẹ́yìn ìbí ni tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó. Àìsàn náà jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ pàtàkì ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ẹ̀ka àgbáyé, ṣùgbọ́n paapapat jùlọ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín tàbí àdúgbò, níbi tí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára ti kéré tí àwọn àṣà ìbímọ aláìní ìmọ́tótó pọ̀ sí. Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé ṣírò pé ní 2018 (ọdún lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ tí àwọn ìṣirò rẹ̀ wà nílẹ̀), àwọn ìkókó 25 000 kú láti ara tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó, àdínkù 88% sí ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ọdún 2000. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ẹ́ jẹ́ pé gbogbo ọ̀ràn tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ń ṣẹlẹ̀ láàrin àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kò tíì gba abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tàbí àwọn àgbàlagbà tí wọn kò tíì ṣe ìmúdójú-ìwọ̀n àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àgbàkún wọn. Àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti àmì àìsàn tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì máa farahàn lásìkò kí àsìkò láti ọjọ́ 3 sí 21 lẹ́yìn tí bakitéríà tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì bá ti wọ inú ara nípasẹ̀ egbò kan.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's the Yoruba translation of the provided text, aiming for clarity and accuracy while considering the context of low-resource language translation. I'll also include some notes afterward explaining choices made.

**Yoruba Translation:**

Tetanus ti o ṣiṣẹ́ lẹ́yìn ìgbé rẹ́, tàbí ní wíwí ósù ńlá tí ìgbé ti parí, ni a ǹ pè ní tetanus ìyàwó, ṣéẹ̀ri tí tetanus ti o ṣiṣẹ́ ní wíwí ọjọ́ 28 tí ọmọdé ní óòní ni a ǹ pè ní tetanus ọmọdé. Àṣàyàn yìí ń tàn káàbò dé ìròyìn ilú ní àwọn ipò̀pọ̀ àgbègbè láìbíjú, óò díẹ̀, ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ó ní ìdàgbádò nípípẹ́ àti àwọn agbo-ilé tó ní ìṣòro, níbí àṣàyàn ìgbésíṣẹpọ̀ níṣíṣú àti àwọn ìṣé àgbàlá tí ó kò ní àwọn ohunèlẹ́. Àwọn ìfamọ́ràn WHO bẹ̀rẹ̀ pé ní ọdún 2018 (ìgbà tí o ṣẹ́dé tí àwọn ìfamọ́ràn wọ́n yà lẹ́nu), ọjọ́ ọmọdé 25,000 ti kú àárọ̀wọ́n ní tetanus ọmọdé, ohun tí ó ń ṣàléfò ní 88% kòjá láti àwọn ìrọ̀yìn tí a rí ní ọdún 2000. Àṣàyàn: Lára àwọn ìwàpọ̀ níbẹ̀, tó ní àwọn ẹ̀ẹ́ tí ó ní tetanus, ó ní àwọn ẹ̀ẹ́ tí kò tẹ́ẹ̀bẹ̀ẹ́ tọ àṣàyàn, àti àwọn ẹ̀ẹ́ tí ó kò ní tọ àṣàyàn àwọn ìgbésíṣẹpọ̀. Àwọn ìwàpọ̀ àti àṣàyàn tí ó ní tetanus yáa nìbẹ̀ láti ọjọ́ 3 sí 21 lẹ́yìn tí àwọn ẹ̀ẹ́ tetanus wọlé inú ara àwọn ẹ̀ẹ́.

**Notes on Translation Choices & Considerations for Low-Resource Languages:**

*   **Terminology:** I've used established Yoruba medical terms where possible.  "Tetanus" is transliterated as "Tetanus" as it's a widely recognized term. "Ìyàwó" is used for maternal, and "ọmọdé" for neonatal.
*   **Sentence Structure:** I've adjusted sentence structure to flow more naturally in Yoruba.  English and Yoruba have different grammatical preferences.
*   **Clarity over Literalness:** In some cases, a more literal translation would be awkward or unclear in Yoruba. I prioritized conveying the meaning accurately.
*   **Numbers:** Numbers are written as numerals (25,000) as is standard in Yoruba writing.
*   **WHO:**  WHO is kept as is, as it's a well-known acronym.
*   **"Low-income countries or districts":** Translated as "àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ó ní ìdàgbádò nípípẹ́ àti àwọn agbo-ilé tó ní ìṣòro" (countries with low income and areas with poverty).
*   **"Unclean birth practices":** Translated as "àwọn ìṣé àgbàlá tí ó kò ní àwọn ohunèlẹ́" (birth practices that lack cleanliness).
*   **"Kept up to date on their booster shots":** Translated as "tí ó kò ní tọ àṣàyàn àwọn ìgbésíṣẹpọ̀" (that have not received booster shots).

**Important Considerations for Further Improvement:**

*   **Contextual Review:** Ideally, a Yoruba-speaking medical professional should review this translation to ensure accuracy and appropriateness for the target audience.
*   **Target Audience:**  Consider the literacy level of the intended audience.  Simplification might be necessary for a general public audience.
*   **Regional Variations:** Yoruba has regional dialects. This translation aims for a generally understandable form, but adjustments might be needed for specific regions.
*   **Glossary:** Creating a glossary of key medical terms in Yoruba would be beneficial for consistency and future translations.

translations.source: Most cases occur within 14 days. Symptoms can include: jaw cramping or the inability to open the mouth muscle spasms often in the back, abdomen and extremities sudden painful muscle spasms often triggered by sudden noises
translations.reference: Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ọ̀ràn máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ láàrin ọjọ́ 14. Àmì àìsàn lè jẹ́: ìwayínpọ tàbí àìlágbára láti yanu ẹ̀gbọ̀n àwọn iṣu ara pàápàá jùlọ lẹ́yìn, ní inú àti àwọn òpin ara. ìnira àwọn ẹ̀gbọ̀n ìṣù ara lójijì èyí tí ariwo òjìji máa ń fà
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's the translation of the provided English text into Yoruba, aiming for clarity and naturalness. I've included some notes afterward explaining choices made.

**Yoruba Translation:**

Ọ̀pọ̀lọ̀ bí ọ́wọ̀n jẹ́ ìròtí láàrin àwọn ọ̀ọ̀rùn tí ó bẹ́ lọ́ọ̀sẹ̀ mọ́ọ́. Àwọn ìwífúnrún le ṣe pẹlú: ìdáná ti paápa jùlọ, tàbí àṣàyìn láìsí ṣí i àgbọn; ìtó-ìtó ara àti ìsòro, àti níbẹ̀ tí ó máa ń ṣí i lára ẹmi, inú àti àwọn apere. Ìtó-ìtó ara tí ó fúnúnú, tí ó máa ń ṣí i nìbẹ̀ tí ó gbọ́ àwọn ohun tó níyòò tó.

**Pronunciation Guide (approximate - Yoruba pronunciation can be nuanced):**

**Notes on Translation Choices:**

*   **"Most cases occur within..."**:  "Ọ̀pọ̀lọ̀ bí ọ́wọ̀n jẹ́..." is a common way to express "most cases" in Yoruba.
*   **"Symptoms can include..."**: "Àwọn ìwífúnrún le ṣe pẹlú..." -  "Ìwífúnrún" is the general term for symptoms. "Le ṣe pẹlú" means "can include."
*   **"Jaw cramping"**: "ìdáná ti paápa jùlọ" - "ìdáná" means cramping, "paápa" means severe/intense, and "jùlọ" means most/very.
*   **"Inability to open the mouth"**: "àṣàyìn láìsí ṣí i àgbọn" - This is a more descriptive way to convey the meaning. "Àṣàyìn" means inability, "láìsí" means without, "ṣí i" means to open, and "àgbọn" means mouth.
*   **"Muscle spasms"**: "ìtó-ìtó ara" - "ìtó" means spasm, and "ara" means body.  Repeating "ìtó" emphasizes the repeated nature of the spasms.
*   **"Often in the back, abdomen and extremities"**: "àti níbẹ̀ tí ó máa ń ṣí i lára ẹmi, inú àti àwọn apere" - This structure is used to indicate location.
*   **"Sudden painful muscle spasms"**: "Ìtó-ìtó ara tí ó fúnúnú" - "fúnúnú" means painful.
*   **"Often triggered by sudden noises"**: "tí ó máa ń ṣí i nìbẹ̀ tí ó gbọ́ àwọn ohun tó níyòò tó" - This is a more idiomatic way to express being triggered by something.

**Important Considerations for Low-Resource Languages:**

*   **Context is Key:**  Without more context about the document's purpose (medical leaflet, general information, etc.), it's difficult to ensure the absolute best word choices.
*   **Regional Variations:** Yoruba has regional dialects. This translation aims for a generally understandable form.
*   **Review by a Native Speaker:**  Ideally, a native Yoruba speaker, especially one with medical knowledge, should review this translation for accuracy and naturalness.
